title: Psalms 128
subtitle: Blessed Are All Who Fear the LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “You Will Eat the Fruit of Your Labour”

heb.paragraph: "Blessed are all who fear (yērēʾ) the LORD, who walk in obedience to him. You will eat the fruit of your labour; blessings and prosperity will be yours" (vv.1–2). The domestic-blessing psalm. Fear of the LORD produces: fruitful labour (v.2), a wife like a fruitful vine (v.3), children like olive shoots (v.3). The home is a garden: vine around the table, olive shoots surrounding it. Fecundity as blessing.

notes.note: "Blessed are all who fear the LORD. You will eat the fruit of your labour." The blessing sequence: fear → obedience → fruitful labour → prosperity. Each step follows from the previous.

"Your wife will be like a fruitful vine within your house; your children will be like olive shoots around your table." The home as garden: vine (productive beauty), olive shoots (new growth). The domestic economy of blessing.

sections.header: Verses 5–6 — “May You See the Prosperity of Jerusalem”

heb.paragraph: "May you live to see your children’s children — peace (shālōm) be on Israel" (v.6). The blessing extends from home to nation. May you see Jerusalem’s prosperity all your days (v.5). May you see grandchildren (v.6). The pilgrimage blessing: personal domestic joy + national prosperity + intergenerational continuity. Shalom closes the psalm, as it closes so many Songs of Ascent.

notes.note: "May you see the prosperity of Jerusalem all the days of your life. May you live to see your children’s children." Three generations of blessing: you, your children, your grandchildren. And over it all: peace on Israel.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in Blessed Are All Who Fear the LORD.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in Blessed Are All Who Fear the LORD.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYou will eat the fruit of your labor; blessings and prosperity will be yours.ESVYou shall eat the fruit of the labor of your hands; you shall be blessed, and it shall be well with you.NIVYes, this will be the blessing for the man who fears the Lord.ESVBehold, thus shall the man be blessed who fears the LORD.

rec.text: ""Blessed are all who fear the LORD. You will eat the fruit of your labour." The blessing sequence: fear → obedience → fruitful labour → prosperity. Each step follows from the previous."

rows.label: Grandchildren’s Peace — 128:5–6
rows.text: "May you see your CHILDREN’S CHILDREN." "PEACE be on Israel." Three generations.

hebtext.note: "Blessed are all who fear (yērēʾ) the LORD, who walk in obedience to him. You will eat the fruit of your labour; blessings and prosperity will be yours" (vv.1–2). The domestic-blessing psalm. Fear of the LORD produces: fruitful labour (v.2), a wife like a fruitful vine (v.3), children like olive shoots (v.3). The home is a garden: vine around the table, olive shoots surrounding it. Fecundity as blessing.

"May you live to see your children’s children — peace (shālōm) be on Israel" (v.6). The blessing extends from home to nation. May you see Jerusalem’s prosperity all your days (v.5). May you see grandchildren (v.6). The pilgrimage blessing: personal domestic joy + national prosperity + intergenerational continuity. Shalom closes the psalm, as it closes so many Songs of Ascent.

themes.note: Covenant peaks: the domestic blessing flows from fear of the LORD. The home as garden. Grandchildren as the ultimate shalom sign.
